primary_diagnosis.name: Neuropathic postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) with dependent acrocyanosis — acute exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: In the neuropathic phenotype of POTS a length-dependent peripheral autonomic (sympathetic) neuropathy impairs reflex vasoconstriction in the dependent circulation, so on standing the leg veins fail to constrict and blood pools in the lower limbs. The venous pooling produces dependent acrocyanosis (the legs turning dark purple/blue) and aching that resolve within seconds of elevating the legs as venous return is restored — the discriminator from an acute thrombotic or arterial occlusion, which does NOT change with posture. To defend cerebral perfusion against the fall in venous return the heart rate rises excessively (an active-stand increment of +63 bpm here, from 72 supine to 135 standing); there is a modest accompanying fall in blood pressure (110/70 supine to 90/60 standing) reflecting the impaired vasoconstriction, but the patient remains well-perfused and mentating without frank hypotensive shock. The excessive tachycardia and cerebral hypoperfusion produce palpitations, tunnel vision, pre-syncope and profound fatigue that all resolve on lying flat. The condition is benign-but-disabling and managed longitudinally in the outpatient setting with volume expansion (fluid and sodium loading), waist-high compression, physical counter-manoeuvres and, if refractory, GP/specialist-initiated pharmacotherapy (e.g. midodrine or fludrocortisone). It is NOT an acute bilateral deep vein thrombosis, arterial limb ischaemia, cardiogenic syncope or hypovolaemic shock — those were the mimics the in-person clinician actively excluded.

differential.diagnosis: Neuropathic POTS-pattern orthostatic intolerance with dependent acrocyanosis (flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Postural tachycardia, pre-syncope and dependent leg discolouration that all resolve on elevation/lying flat, on a background of longstanding specialist-investigated orthostatic intolerance — the classic neuropathic POTS pattern.

Bilateral painful discoloured legs raise DVT as a can't-miss mimic — must be actively screened (symmetry, posture-dependence, calf tenderness/swelling, pulses) before attributing to dysautonomia; cannot obtain compression ultrasound via telehealth.

A dusky, painful limb warrants screening for a fixed pale, cold, pulseless, numb limb (a genuine vascular emergency) — distinguished here by symmetry, warmth, intact pulses and full resolution on elevation.
Racing heart and near-collapse could reflect a primary arrhythmia — must be excluded before attributing to dysautonomia; a resting ECG cannot be obtained via telehealth.
Presyncope with tachycardia can be volume depletion — needs the hydration/urine-output screen and an orthostatic assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should recognise a postural-intolerance pattern AND keep the serious mimics (bilateral DVT, arterial ischaemia, arrhythmia, volume depletion) explicitly on the list to be screened — not leap to either an emergency or a dismissal.

differential.diagnosis: Neuropathic POTS-pattern orthostatic intolerance with dependent acrocyanosis (flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral, symmetrical discolouration that resolves within seconds of elevation (DI-002), no single pale/cold/pulseless/numb limb and no unilateral swelling (DI-004), no chest pain or resting palpitations (DI-005), tolerating fluids and passing urine (DI-007), longstanding specialist-investigated postural intolerance (DI-006), near-faints without true loss of consciousness (DI-008) — a benign chronic dysautonomia flare with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen.

Symmetrical, posture-dependent discolouration that resolves instantly on elevation, with soft non-tender calves, equal palpable pedal pulses and a negative Homan's sign on in-person exam — not a thrombotic pattern (DVT does not change with limb position).

Both limbs warm with intact pulses and full colour return on elevation; no fixed pale, cold, pulseless or numb limb (DI-004) — arterial occlusion excluded, but retained as a mandatory safety-net trigger if a single limb changes.

Lowered by the strictly-postural pattern and absence of chest pain or resting palpitations — but a resting ECG to exclude a primary arrhythmia cannot be done remotely, so it remains an outpatient investigation item, not an emergency.
Still taking fluids and passing urine, not vomiting (DI-007); the in-person clinician found moist mucous membranes and normal skin turgor — severe volume depletion excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen the leading diagnosis is a benign neuropathic POTS-pattern flare managed in the outpatient setting. Escalating this baseline to ED/000 because 'telehealth cannot get an ultrasound / ECG / orthostatic vitals' is the over-triage error this case is built to catch — the remaining imaging/ECG/autonomic testing is arranged as prompt outpatient workup, not an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Neuropathic POTS with dependent acrocyanosis — acute exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: recognise the known benign chronic cause, arrange prompt GP review + autonomic/specialist workup, give fluid/salt loading, waist-high compression and counter-manoeuvre advice with fall precautions, and attach mandatory safety-netting for genuine deterioration (true syncope, a single ischaemic limb, chest pain).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final action is recognise + refer + safety-net at T2. Formal autonomic (active-stand / tilt) testing and a resting ECG are appropriate outpatient investigations; they do not need to be obtained emergently given the negative acute red-flag screen.

red_flags.description: Excessive postural tachycardia (heart racing on standing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The core POTS-pattern finding; excessive but strictly postural tachycardia. Concerning in isolation but reassuring once shown to be posture-dependent and to resolve on lying flat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If mis-read as a primary tachyarrhythmia, leads to unnecessary emergency escalation; if dismissed, misses the need for outpatient cardiac/autonomic workup.

red_flags.description: Pre-syncope with tunnel vision on standing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cerebral hypoperfusion on standing; drives the fall risk and warrants fall precautions, but is posture-dependent and relieved by lying flat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the fall risk could lead to a traumatic fall / head injury at home, especially as the patient is alone.

red_flags.description: Bilateral dependent acrocyanosis (legs turning dark purple) with aching, resolving on elevation
red_flags.clinical_significance: The venous-pooling sign of neuropathic POTS. Alarming and DVT-mimicking, but benign once shown to be bilateral, symmetrical, warm, pulsatile and fully reversible on elevation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If mis-read as acute bilateral DVT or limb ischaemia, leads to unnecessary emergency escalation; if dismissed, misses the diagnosis and the fall risk.

red_flags.clinical_significance: No chest pain — argues against an acute coronary or thromboembolic cause. If chest pain develops it becomes a T5 (000) trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not screening for chest pain would miss a genuine cardiac / PE emergency if it arose.

red_flags.description: A single limb becoming fixed pale, cold, pulseless and numb (acute limb ischaemia) — or a unilateral hot, swollen, tender calf (DVT)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Both legs are symmetrical, warm, pulsatile and reverse fully on elevation — arterial ischaemia and DVT are excluded. A change to a single fixed pale/cold/pulseless/numb limb, or a unilateral hot swollen tender calf, would be a genuine vascular emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a true unilateral limb-ischaemia or DVT event would risk limb loss or pulmonary embolism.

red_flags.description: True loss of consciousness (syncope) — as opposed to the pre-syncope seen so far
red_flags.clinical_significance: Only pre-syncope so far (near-faints, no LOC, no fall). A true syncopal event would raise concern for a cardiac cause and change the tier.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net means a future true syncope or fall with injury would not trigger escalation.

red_flags.description: Orthostatic hypotension with shock / severe dehydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Standing blood pressure falls only modestly (90/60) with the patient well-perfused and mentating; moist mucous membranes and normal skin turgor on exam; still tolerating fluids and passing urine — hypotensive shock and severe volume depletion excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing true hypovolaemia / shock would under-treat a volume emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: DVT / limb-ischaemia over-triage on the purple legs
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the dramatic bilateral purple legs and the telephone-triage framing ('rule out acute bilateral DVT / circulatory collapse') into emergency escalation. The discriminators — bilateral symmetry, instant reversal on elevation, soft non-tender warm calves with intact pulses — identify benign dependent acrocyanosis, not thrombosis.

Reasoning 'I can't get a leg ultrasound / ECG / orthostatic vitals remotely, so I must send her to ED/000.' This is the primary error the case tests: with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen the imaging/ECG/autonomic testing are prompt OUTPATIENT workup, not an emergency.

Treating the near-faints (no loss of consciousness, no fall/injury, patient lies down before going down) as true syncopal events and escalating on that basis. True LOC would change the tier; it is explicitly absent here.

The opposite failure: dismissing it as 'just a POTS flare / anxiety' with pure self-care and no in-person review, no autonomic/cardiac workup, no fall precautions (the patient is alone) and no safety-netting — missing the fall risk and the small residual chance of an arrhythmic or vascular cause.

Adopting the source note's low-dose tadalafil ('endothelial support') as a telehealth prescription — it is not an evidence-based first-line POTS therapy and is not appropriate for a telehealth AI Doctor to initiate. Midodrine / fludrocortisone are GP/specialist-initiated after formal assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Active-stand / lying-and-standing postural blood pressure and heart rate (measured in-person: BP 110/70 supine to 90/60 standing, HR 72 to 135, +63 bpm) — unless the patient has a validated home BP cuff and heart-rate device
Calf palpation for tenderness/swelling, equality and warmth of the limbs, and palpable pedal pulses (in-person exam found soft non-tender calves, equal palpable pedal pulses, negative Homan's sign)
Direct assessment of the acrocyanosis reversing on leg elevation on examination
Mucous-membrane hydration and skin-turgor assessment (in-person exam found moist membranes, normal turgor)
Compression ultrasound of the legs to exclude DVT
Resting 12-lead ECG to exclude a primary arrhythmia
Formal autonomic function testing (active-stand or head-up tilt table)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign neuropathic POTS-pattern flare and manage at T2: prompt GP review (within 24–48 hours) with a resting ECG and referral for autonomic testing (and leg ultrasound only if the vascular picture were not clearly benign), immediate fluid and sodium loading plus waist-high compression and physical counter-manoeuvres, fall precautions (shower chair, avoid unsupported standing, arrange help as the patient is alone), and MANDATORY safety-netting to 000/ED for a single fixed pale/cold/pulseless/numb limb or a unilateral hot swollen tender calf, true loss of consciousness, chest pain, or inability to keep fluids down.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign dysautonomia pattern given the negative acute red-flag screen. The real risks are (1) OVER-action — defaulting to ED/000 for a suspected clot or because vitals/ECG cannot be obtained remotely; and (2) UNDER-action — dismissing it without arranging the ECG/autonomic workup, fall precautions or safety-netting.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Sustained heart-rate rise of +63 bpm within the first minutes of standing (72 to 135 bpm), with only a modest fall in blood pressure (110/70 to 90/60) and no hypotensive shock — consistent with a POTS pattern (adult criterion sustained rise ≥+30 bpm) on the in-person active-stand measurement.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a neuropathic POTS-pattern orthostatic intolerance; confirmation still requires formal outpatient autonomic testing and a resting ECG to exclude an arrhythmia.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus on POTS 2015
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: POTS is defined by a sustained heart-rate increment (≥+30 bpm adults) on standing with chronic orthostatic symptoms; the neuropathic subtype features dependent acrocyanosis from impaired peripheral vasoconstriction; first-line management is volume expansion (fluid and salt), compression, physical counter-manoeuvres and graded exercise, with pharmacotherapy reserved for refractory cases.

Orthostatic intolerance / POTS is managed longitudinally in primary care with non-pharmacological measures first; escalate only for genuine red flags (true syncope, chest pain, a single ischaemic limb, shock).
